name: Performance Tuning with sysctl
description: Tune kernel parameters using sysctl to optimize network performance, file descriptor limits, and memory management.

steps.title: View current kernel parameters
steps.description: List all sysctl parameters and their current values.

steps.title: Check current file descriptor limits
steps.description: View the maximum number of open file descriptors.
steps.tip: fs.file-max is the system-wide limit. ulimit -n is the per-process limit.

steps.title: Optimize TCP settings for high-traffic servers
steps.description: Increase TCP connection backlog and enable TCP optimizations.
steps.command: sudo sysctl -w net.core.somaxconn=65535 && sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=65535
steps.warning: These changes are temporary and lost on reboot. Add them to /etc/sysctl.d/ to persist.

steps.title: Adjust swappiness for server workloads
steps.description: Reduce swappiness to prefer keeping application memory in RAM.
steps.tip: Default is 60. For database servers, 10 is common. For desktops, 60 is fine.

steps.title: Persist sysctl changes across reboots
steps.description: Save tuning parameters to a config file so they survive reboots.
steps.command:
echo 'net.core.somaxconn=65535
vm.swappiness=10
fs.file-max=2097152' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-performance.conf && sudo sysctl --system

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Performance Tuning Audit
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Current Performance Parameters ==="
echo "Swappiness:        $(sysctl -n vm.swappiness)"
echo "File max:          $(sysctl -n fs.file-max)"
echo "Somaxconn:         $(sysctl -n net.core.somaxconn)"
echo "TCP backlog:       $(sysctl -n net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog)"
echo "TCP keepalive:     $(sysctl -n net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time)s"
echo "Ulimit (soft):     $(ulimit -Sn)"
echo "Ulimit (hard):     $(ulimit -Hn)"

echo ""
echo "=== Memory ==="
echo "Total:    $(free -h | awk '/Mem:/{print $2}')"
echo "Used:     $(free -h | awk '/Mem:/{print $3}')"
echo "Cache:    $(free -h | awk '/Mem:/{print $6}')"
echo "Swap:     $(free -h | awk '/Swap:/{print $3}') used of $(free -h | awk '/Swap:/{print $2}')"

echo ""
echo "=== Recommended Server Tuning ==="
echo "sudo sysctl -w vm.swappiness=10"
echo "sudo sysctl -w net.core.somaxconn=65535"
echo "sudo sysctl -w fs.file-max=2097152"
echo "sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=65535"
echo ""
echo "Persist: Add to /etc/sysctl.d/99-performance.conf"

faq.question: Is it safe to change sysctl parameters?
faq.answer: Most network and file descriptor limits are safe to increase. Be careful with memory-related parameters (vm.*) on systems with limited RAM. Always test changes in a non-production environment first.

faq.question: How do I revert a sysctl change?
faq.answer: Set the parameter back to its original value with 'sysctl -w param=original_value'. To find defaults, check 'sysctl -a' on a fresh system or the kernel documentation.
